you. Yep. Yep. Get the commissioner on the line. Will they fit? There's a lot of them out there. Yeah, there are. It's like, I reckon there's 100 people out there. I don't think they're all coming here though, hopefully. They're in. Oh. Only the ones in uniform. F21. Ah. Duncan got through the security.

Hello. Oh, gosh. So Dan, we have two hours on police, so- One second. Oh, thank you. It is also- So that means he's left six minutes. No, it's also at the discretion of the chair. No. You forget to say that bit. Well, hang on. Specific standing. Can I ask the photographer? Can I ask the photographer, you're not doing that in here, please.

Yes, he's allowed to. It's chocolate. You're not going to do that in here. Oh, geez. You can stand up here if you like. Duncan, I needed you yesterday and Is that sort of a benefit? Where are they then? You can be in here, but I don't want you running around chasing. Can't roam around. Has to be in a fixed position.

I don't want you running around chasing somebody. All that's set up. I was like, um, hang on. I think I should've been out there. Karen's the usual one. Look at that face. This doesn't work. You could be here. Oh, hi. Hello. Tell me about it. Would you like some water?

No worries. A little thingy. Say that like I type anything. My little thingy. Yeah, I think so. Yeah. And so Andrew, I'll just write like four or five and then I'll go. Yeah, I do that. I always do that. I always do that. That's right. No, I want it green because it's going to do a very swap.

Oh, no, not on this topic. Oh, okay. Righto, everyone. Not that we can hear you. Okay. First of all, I'd like to thank the minister for turning up again today. We've had you the other day on a different matter. Also, I'd like to acknowledge the commissioner, Richard Chambers.

You're in London, I think. We're aware that you're going overseas, so away on important police business. But I'm very glad that you're up, probably in the middle of the night doing this conference. Just want to acknowledge all the other police in the room. We've got two hours on police this morning, but we've got an hour and a half with you, Minister.

I'm going to let you do some introductory comments, and then obviously we'll get into it. Well, yeah, thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you to you and the committee. I'll allow the, I've got the leadership team here from DC to allow go introduce yourself and from the police and, obviously Mark Mitchell, Minister of Police and also Minister for the Serious Fraud Office. I've got a very short opening comments. I'll pass the others to introduce themselves.
I'm the Deputy Commissioner, Mike Pennant. Good. Hi. Good morning. Andrea Conlon, Chief Operating Officer, Police. Karen Chang, Director of the Serious Fraud Office. Cool.

The government's expectations have been for police to focus on core policing, hold offenders to account, and support victims whose sense of safety and security has been robbed by crime. We have put in the resources, investment, and the laws needed to back up our expectations, and that focus is working.

Wherever I go, people tell me that they see their local police out and about on the streets, and they feel safer as a result. Major centers and urban areas have beat teams and gang disruption units. The Gangs Act has been a major success in giving police the powers to ensure gangs no longer have license to intimidate and take over our provincial towns.

Ram raids have plummeted and retail crime is taken seriously, benefiting workers, businesses, and the general public. Boy racers no longer have it easy, and legislation before Parliament will give police more powers to deal with their reckless and antisocial behavior on our roads. Police have made excellent progress on the key government targets that should continue into the coming year.

Serious and repeat youth offending is down by 25% compared to 2023, and there were 49,000 fewer victims of violent crime in the year to October 2025 than two years previously. As a result, people feel safer. Law and order has dropped well down the list of issues that are a concern to the public in recent surveys such as Ipsos that has been shown.

That has not happened by accident. That is a result of excellent work by police, as well as this government's investment to ensure the frontline has the resources and powers to deliver on it. That support has continued in this year's budget, which delivers 391 million in further investment for police to support the frontline and the communities that they serve.

Budget 2026 includes investments in technology, property, and 53.5 million funding for cost pressures to support the frontline. The government is delivering on upgrading and replacing stations that our frontline police across the regions need. I went to the opening of the new Taradale Police Station earlier this month, a base that was built on time, under budget, and allows police to better service the wider Hawke's Bay region.

This year's budget delivers funding for further much-needed replacements for the Whanganui Police Station and Greymouth Police Station and custody facility, and that is good news both for the police and the public in those areas. The budget also delivers funding to replace the automated biometric identification system. That is key technology which is crucial for many areas of core policing. The replacement technology for dealing with biometric information such as fingerprints will be more effective, more efficient,

and result in faster outcomes for victims. The budget also delivers on the coalition agreement by providing 8.3 million of funding for those groups which do a great job in our communities to help the police keep people safe. This funding for community patrols, Neighborhood Support NZ, and Pacific Wardens gives them certainty and allows them to continue and expand their services.

There is still much work to do, but I want to acknowledge the commissioner for his work over the last year, his support for the frontline, his focus on building trust and confidence by ensuring police deliver for victims and the public. It is well-known police has had to deal with some challenges over the last year. The response to those challenges, in particular those highlighted in the IPCA's November 2025 report, will continue to be a focus for the government. I'm confident police is on solid footing to address those issues. The new executive and leadership

teams are now in place. I welcome the commissioner's work to strengthen integrity and his targets around trust and confidence, public place violence, retail crime resolutions, and the service people get from police. The performance improvement review requested by the commissioner has given police an independent overview of the challenges, and useful guidance on what is needed over the short and longer terms.

There is good awareness of the challenges that lie ahead and a plan for the future. In particular, the intended police capability plan is set to be a valuable exercise in planning for the future investments police require. The government will continue to work with police to ensure it is well-placed to meet those challenges. Thank you.

Right. Thank you. I'm going to start with Honorable Jenny Anderson. Thank you very much, Mr. Chair, and to Minister and police staff. Minister, when are you going to reach the 500 more police that you promised at the last election? Well, the police are doing an outstanding job. They've got a really strong pipeline of people wanting to join the police.

We've got people coming back from overseas, and the numbers that I see and that are being tracked by both myself and my Associate Minister of Police, Casey Costello, shows that the police are on target to deliver the 500 towards the end of this year. You've given lots of other dates in the past. Why won't you give the public one now?

Well, I've been very clear that the police are showing a clear motivation to deliver on that target and, especially with the opening of the new training facility in Auckland, they've got a strong pipeline of new police officers, new people wanting to join the police. We've got a large number of people wanting to rejoin the police, which I take as a very good signal, and we've got people coming back from Australia.

Why did you promise 500 more police in two years then? Because that was a coalition commitment. Right. Do you think that the police wages are a factor in why you are struggling to recruit? And if so, are you looking to repay the risk, as many police are asking? Well, on a personal level, I always want to see any of our first responders paid as much as we can afford as a country.

There is currently bargaining underway, and it would be totally inappropriate for me to comment on that. So all members of Parliament have received numerous submissions from frontline police. I'd just like to read one out of the hundreds we've got. Quote, "We are increasingly understaffed, routinely exposed to serious violence, and expected to absorb the cost of that risk.

Like many colleagues, I work long hours, take danger home with me, and still struggle with the cost of living. The reality is pay no longer reflects what we are being asked to do. Will you show up for us? Will you repay the risk?" Do you have a response to that, Minister? Yes. I'm very proud of the work that our frontline police officers do. I'm very aware of the challenges that they have, and that's why this government has invested enormous amounts of money into our police and into our frontline to support them, to give them more powers.

Since we've come into government, there's at least an additional 250 police officers already out there on the front line. We have freed up police officers from being mental health workers. So the police are in a very good position in terms of front line strength. There's more police to come, and there's been an enormous investment into the front line. When I became police minister, the police were going broke.

The previous government had not invested in their cost pressures, they had not invested in their core, and they were in danger of breaching their appropriation. We immediately invested $120 million into police to stabilize them. And this government is a law and order government, and it remains committed to continuing to invest, and that's exactly what we've done. I'm pleased you raised that, Minister, because you might like to explain to the public why, for the second budget in a row, you appear to be paying for police wages out of time-limited funding.

Why haven't you baselined the amount of money required to pay the wages of front line police? No, we've provided money in this budget for collective bargaining, which is underway, and it would be totally inappropriate for me to comment or talk to that. So I've received that information through written PQs from you that the $50 million time-limited funding specified in the budget, $30 million is earmarked for wages out of that.

Why have you not baselined the amount of money required to pay the wages of front Well, I'm very clear that we have provided money in this budget for collective bargaining, and it is up to police then to enter into goodwill collective bargaining with the association and the police guild, and that is underway at the moment, and it would be totally inappropriate for me to comment any further on that.

I'm not asking you to comment on the bargaining. Okay, one more after this. One more. I want to know why are police wages not part of the police baseline in the budget? Well, police have been funded for the... I'll say it a third time. Police have been funded in this budget round for the collective bargaining, which they're in now, which they're negotiating now.

Why have you not baselined that money? Why is it time limited each budget? They've got the money available for their collective bargaining. What happens when it runs out? No, that's it. This is last one, Jenny. You got a question or is that- Oh, okay. All right, I'll go to- Thanks, Chair Thanks very much, Minister, for joining us this morning, and thanks for the commitment that you've made to our community across the country like Whanganui.

There has been some media report, though, about the fact that the Whanganui Police Station, for example, was something that was already promised. Can you comment on the journey that's got us to today? Well, like a lot of things, there might've been a public announcement about the Whanganui Police Station, but there was no funding for it. And so this government has found the funding to actually be able to deliver the Whanganui Police Station, and we recognize, police recognize that as a priority because that's part of a broader

justice precinct that has been delivered there. So under your leadership, the community can have confidence that that money is actually allocated now and- That money is allocated, it is ring-fenced, and police will start working on that immediately. Supplementary, Chair. Can you just also tell us about the other funding that's gone into that community, to improve the police facilities in Whanganui over the last couple of years that have led into this decision being made today?

Indeed, sweetie. Sure. So we'd previously received funding for the custody care facility, and the works on that is underway. So that is a shared facility with the Ministry of Justice, which will be operational in time for the new courthouse. The new funding received through Budget '26 is specifically for the station aspect of the hub, which we will now commence Fantastic. Thank you.

All right. Good. I'm going to go to Timipone. Timipone. Morena. All right. I've got a couple topics that I'm interested in today. Mainly the move on orders. The other one is children being held in- Just make sure there's some reference to the budget. Yep. All right. Police held in... Sorry, children held in police cells, which might overlap a little bit with and the stalking law and just how you're preparing to implement that, and what costs have been associated with that.

So starting with the move on orders, that's a piece of legislation that'll come into effect before the next budget. The regulatory impact statement doesn't outline what the associated costs are for police in implementing that piece of legislation. I couldn't find specificity within the budget in terms of how much that's going to cost to implement, but I did appreciate the police's comments in the RIS about the bill around their preference for a infringement fee option. Anyway, that's all right. We got what we've got.

But how much is that going to cost to enforce the move on orders, and how is the police preparing for those? Yeah, police obviously will have to have a close look at that. In my view, probably it shouldn't be very much because there's a cost associated with police time right now, in dealing with the social disorder and the issues associated with rough sleepers and people that are choosing to come out and live on the street.

As we both know that not only do they get used by gangs to peddle drugs or behave antisocially towards the people that, the residents or the people that are coming to the CBDs and want to enjoy the CBD, they're also at risk themselves. We've had really clear advice from St.

John's that, and we saw a very clear example of that in Christchurch about a month ago, where a rough sleeper was stabbed. So we don't want people living on the streets. The move on orders, currently in this country, there's no ability to be able to move people from living on the streets. That's what the move on order is about.

The police will be-- It's about rough sleepers, people that are choosing to come and actually live on the streets. Many of those do have somewhere safe to go and sleep or stay. The ones that are in genuine homelessness- I think that recently I was up in Auckland and met with- Mm... the City Mission, and they've brought a couple of really good policy ideas around how we can perhaps, and this is something that we're looking at, it's not something that's been announced as policy yet-... because it's going through the process of Select Committee, et cetera.

But, requirement to engage. Mm. So, if the police are dealing with someone that doesn't have somewhere to go, doesn't have somewhere safe to stay, then there's a requirement for the government to engage and identify what are their needs, if they're genuine homelessness needs, and then meet those needs. And I think we'd get a nice balance around the move on order. I thought that was actually some really good ideas that were thought forward by the people that are also remembering that police are dealing with rough sleepers all the time. So is City Mission.

They're the same people. Yeah. And I think it was really awesome that you went up and spent time at our Keep Egg Make Change with some of those organizations that do support people who are rough sleeping. How does the, I guess, budget commitment, which we don't fully understand yet, and the policy rationale that you just outlined, align with the fact that public disorder is at a 10-year low according to police data?

Yeah. Because the police are coming, public disorder overall, across the board, it's a broad spectrum. So boy races, gangs, note no provincial towns, general retail violence, violence associated with on-license premises, those sorts of things. Yep, all of that's coming down. And actually, the rough sleeping situation is, I believe anyway, is starting to improve as well. In fact, my stakeholder group that we're meeting regularly in Auckland to try and fix the issues that we had in the CBD, Chloe was a part of that.

We started to see some real gains, but we have got a really difficult, stubborn group there that just insist that they want to come out and live on the street for whatever reason. And, so that is what really what the move on orders are there for, so that we can... Some of them don't want to engage with central government.

I heard that when I was up at the City Mission and in other groups, that actually there's a s- deep suspicion of central government and local government, and they don't want to engage. We have to have some sort of mechanism to get them to engage, and then try and build out that trust, and actually the move on order will be a very good mechanism of doing that. Mm. And that's why I think sitting on the other side, it'd be a very good idea for the government to have a required to engage rules in there too.

Mm, mm. So that the genuine-... the people that are genuinely homeless, we can actually identify what the issues are, and then go about actually fixing those. And, as you know, you, because you're the person who, I believe signs off when we meet with police, so as you know, you've signed off a couple meetings that I've had with- Yeah... senior police officials. And some of the conversations I've had, my understanding was that they were seeking a move on order in the first place for drunk people who stumbled out of the club at

3:00 a.m. and were just a complete nuisance to the public, and started fights and were disorderly, as opposed to it being a tool used against people who were rough sleeping or homeless. So it seems like the purpose that police wanted these move on orders, maybe has been a bit contorted by politics. No, I don't think that. I definitely- Maybe Sunny Kaushal influence?

No, I think that's definitely an issue, though. Is that people that come into the CBDs, and then want to cause trouble, they take up a lot of police time. So it's good that the move on orders will be able to assist with that as well. I sort of want to get rid of this sort of perception that's trying to be built, and this myth that the police want to go out and target people. They don't.

They actually just want to help people. That's what they're motivated for. That's if you go to a graduation, see our young police officers graduating, they're in their public service because they want to help and support the community that they're in. And they don't want to-- no one wants to see homelessness. And they'll do everything that they can to be able to assist and support, making sure if someone is identified with a, and they're homeless and they need some support, then that can be delivered. They can't deliver that.

We need the rest of the government to respond and be able to actually deliver that for them. Right. That's my last question, is just around children under 18 years old being held in police cells. So, and hope there might be someone in the room that might be able to assist with this, or it might be more of a Oranga Tamariki question. But, I've noticed that the percentage of young people who are held in police custody for more than, sorry, for less than 24 hours and for more than 24 hours seems to be increasing. But they're... I can see Oranga Tamariki has changed the target.

So they used to record the number of kids held in police custody, which was 140, 186 last year, and now they've changed it to at least 85%. So they're just becoming a, they're changing their measures. And I guess I was interested in, have the police been liaising with Oranga Tamariki, and what's happening in that space to make sure that kids aren't held in cells- Yeah... that were not designed for them?

Yeah. So police don't, as a whole, don't want to have youth offenders in the cells. And in terms of the relationship between police and Oranga Tamariki, I think it's the best and strongest it's been for a long time. They are working very closely together. We do rely on Oranga Tamariki to make a determined response in terms of being able to actually house and look after these young people.

But I would say that the, when we do have young people in our cells, they're well looked after. Our authorized officers are some of the best in the world. And often they're safer there than they would be back out on the streets, re-offending, putting the public at risk and themselves. But I acknowledge that we don't want to have them in police cells, and there's been an enormous amount of work going in to make sure that they spend as little time there and they're transitioned into a Oranga Tamariki facility.

But- I don't know. Do you- If, yeah, if I may comment, Minister, if that's all right. You can hear me okay? Yes, we can. Yeah. Okay, look, and I'm sorry that I can't be there with you. I'm representing New Zealand at the Five Eyes law enforcement meeting, as the deputy chair as well. Look, the executive monitor young people in custody every single day.

It's one of the things we talk about at our 8:00 morning briefings. We want to ensure that young people are kept out of our cells, but for a range of reasons. And I will call on Tasha Penny shortly just to talk a little bit about the sort of work that we do with Oranga Tamariki on a daily basis.

But we are very conscious. We all support the need to keep our young people out of police cells, and that is why we work so hard. Daily we review it, and most recently, this morning even, dialing into the executives meeting here from London, and I see there's one young person.

Well, we work hard every day to make sure that they are taken out of custody as soon as possible and put into an appropriate location. But Tasha, would you like to add anything further? Yeah. Thanks, Commissioner. Kia ora. So like the commissioner said, we do monitor it every day. I think more importantly, we've just had a big look at it. So whilst, if we compare the last 12 months to the previous, we're actually holding fewer children in custody, however, for longer. And the longer looks like one hour.

So that's what it is, the median average. What we do though is we are working very closely with Oranga Tamariki. Two of us have just recently finished long-term secondments there. The ones that we're holding for longer, and I think you are really acutely aware of this, have really complex needs. Like everyone said, we know that it's not ideal to hold young people there. As a result of that, we have daily meetings with Oranga Tamariki. We say, "We've held them for this long.

Where are the beds?" And the issue is when they need to go into youth justice secure facilities and the bed rates. But we are working probably closer than we ever have before around this issue, monitoring the bed rates, ensuring that the young people are looked after no matter what care they're in and are going through. So it's something we challenge ourselves collectively about all the time.

Right. I'm going to go to a supplementary from Rima Nakay. Tasha, thank you very much. Good morning, everyone. Thank you. Good morning, Becky. You just said that you're working closer than ever before on this. Why is that? And what's prompted this extra closeness of work with our children in custody?

So, youth crime have been a long-term ... There is no color to this issue. It's been a long-term social issue. But what we are doing, if we look at fast track for the 10 to 13 year olds that was implemented in 2024, and the significant results that have had. So that's that early intervention getting in, and it actually all is about prevention. The serious and serious youth persistent offenders, we're really having a look at that.

And like we've said, you're not going to get anyone who thinks that a young person should be held in our custody, but the reality is sometimes they need to. And the more effective our partnership is with Oranga Tamariki, and the more we work together with them to make sure they get the right help, the right assessment, then that's what we need to do. I actually could talk about this for a long time, so I won't.

Okay. But that's what I mean. We're really collectively... And this is not a police issue. This is a far broader issue, and it's actually not just a government issue e- either, as we see from the multitude of NGOs and initiatives we've got going on. We've piloted a new pilot that's just getting off the ground in Christchurch around youth crime that already is showing really good results. And I think the key thing is we're co-locating around youth crime with Oranga Tamariki, and we are working together as a team, because that is the only way we're actually going to make

long-term impacts with this issue. Yeah. Yeah, and I think there's just like, I think the Assistant Commissioner just made a really good point, is that both agencies are getting stronger because there's been that cross-pollination. So you've got Assistant Commissioner Penny who's actually been seconded into OT. So they understand the demands and pressures.

And with support from the government, we're working much closer together to actually deliver exactly what... But it's going to take time, without a doubt. All right. We've got decades of- I think we're going to go onto a new topic from Honorable Janine Anstey. Thank you, Mr. Chair. The Policing Amendment Bill gives police powers to collect intelligence, but the Privacy Commissioner has come out very strongly saying that this legislation should not proceed without the appropriate checks in place- So just make sure that that's there

... which is the IT infrastructure to recall, store, and destroy images. The last reports on that, that that was going to be sought in the budget. I can't find that in the budget. Is it your intention to proceed with that legislation against the wishes of the Privacy Commissioner without the IT infrastructure in place?

Yeah, I don't. I'd have to... I don't want you to mischaracterize the Privacy Commissioner. We have been working very closely with him and his office. Of course, they have a critical role to play, but we are reinstating powers that police always had and are critically important for public safety. We make no apology for that.

And yes, we will be proceeding with it. And police have shown that they are responsible, that they can actually- Good morning... that they can use their existing systems to be able to support the work that's being done, and that we're investing heavily into their systems. But I don't know if, Deputy, you wanted to comment any further on that?

No, we will continue to invest and we will look at how we implement. There is quite considerable discussions to be going on around the world, so that is still Before the House. Welcome in Is there any line in the budget you can identify that enables funding for the requisite IT infrastructure for the storage, destruction, and recollection of images?

Right. Sure. So I guess the main thing here is this is about restoring the store- Can I get you to just pull the- Sorry... microphone towards both of you. Thanks. Yeah Is that better? Yeah, just as close as you can get it. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah, no worries. So this is around retaining the right to store images that we previously had. So we are confident that our existing systems will have the capacity for that.

We are also working on improvements, particularly to our digital mobility system, which is where we believe most of the images will be taken. If you'd like more detail, I would need to call up our Chief Information Officer, Matt Winter, who could talk to that in more depth, if you would like. Well, I still haven't kind of had the answer to the question, that the Privacy Commissioner has ruled that you don't currently have the existing IT infrastructure to be able to meet the requirements.

Well, that's the Privacy Commissioner's view. And I don't see, and there's no money in the budget. The last- Well, I'm sorry, but- If I could just be allowed to ask the question... number one, I'd like to see the full context of the Privacy Commissioner's views on that. I can read it out to you, if you like. I can table it. Well, that'd be great. But the second part is this. That is the Privacy Commissioner's view. He doesn't run the police systems, the police do.

So- You don't agree with the Privacy Commissioner's ruling on it? So I believe that the police have got a long track record of being able to manage their own systems. Yeah. Point of order, Chair. What's that? It's no public document. It's not. It's a submission. It's publicly released. It's a submission on the bill. No, it's- It's a submission on the bill... any other I've sent.

Just on, in that document. Public interest. Just that, just- It's a submission on the bill, and it's publicly available. Just listen. Just stop. I'm just saying you can only refer to that document, so be careful about it. Do you disagree with the Privacy Commissioner's submission that your legislation should not proceed given the fact you do not have the requisite- I'm not commenting on the Privacy Commissioner's submission.

What I'm saying is that I've got full confidence that the police are able to deliver, and will stick within the rules of the law, and will be able to respond to the policy. So the Privacy Commissioner says that you do not. Are you publicly disagreeing with the ruling of the Privacy Commissioner? Oh, I just don't- Look, I think we've heard your- The police don't comment on the Privacy Commissioner's systems.

You just did Okay. No. I said that I don't agree. If that is the Privacy Commissioner's position, then I don't agree with it. I think the police have got the systems, they've got the capability to be able to meet the need. The police are very, very conscious of their responsibility to actually carry out strictly the intent of the law.

And this is an important amendment bill that provides them the powers that they always had, and that they lost through a, um- Right. You're going to need to- One more question. I won't- Judge one... so the last comment that your office gave is that you need to find a budget bid. This work is likely to be significant and will likely require a business case to be developed for consideration in a future budget cycle. That is an official paper written about six months ago about this project.

Can you please tell me if you have a business case, and if there is a budget bid for the IT required to meet the Privacy Commissioner's ruling? So the police are doing an enormous amount of work around business cases for both physical infrastructure and IT infrastructure, because it's been under-invested for decades, and- Exactly... and we are catching up on that. That's why we're making significant investment through this budget round. If you want specific information on it, because you've been offered, so if you're really genuinely-

Yeah. I want to hear that... if you're really genuinely interested in this- Okay... why don't we bring Matt forward so that he can speak to that? Thanks. We'll just hear from the gentleman at the back.

Kia ora. Matt Winter, Chief Information Officer for New Zealand Police. So the key part that we're focusing on is the digital notebook, which is a relatively new capability for our officers, which works on their mobile phones. So it should literally replace the physical ones they carry today, but with a whole lot more capability. And that's the main way that we're going to manage ingesting the photos, tagging them so we know the context within which they're taken, and managing them through their life cycle.

So that's different from what we've had before. And we've done that through a rolling program of work we have to enhance our mobile applications. I understand that, but my understanding, the digital notebook is a digital way of what it would be the old police notebook. That doesn't seem to have the IT infrastructure and capability to store, destroy, and recall existing images. Am I correct?

That is what we're developing, and it... So it didn't used to, but we have now developed the features to be able to take the photos into the digital notebook. So that didn't used to be there. That gets tagged with metadata, so we know the context of those photos as well, and then we'll be building the life cycle management for the photo storage that sits behind it as well. That's great. So all of the photos that already exist, is that system able to do that for all of the photos that haven't yet met the requirements of the Privacy

Commissioner? No, this is a forward-looking piece of infrastructure. So it won't do the re- Right. Last one. Okay, cool. Premier, sup. No, I've got Rima Nakli. Was it sup? Yeah. Supplementary, please. Minister, so with this work that we're doing on the legislation that the Honorable Ginny Andersen has just been going on about, what is the problem we're trying to solve?

Oh, it's just a straight out policing problem, where police historically had the ability to- Okay... collect images when it related to offending- Okay... for intelligence purposes. That helped obviously investigations and solving crimes, keeping the public safe. So they lost that ability, and we reinstated it. No, it's because they were taking tens of thousands of pictures of kids.

Sorry. Well, I'm- Of kids... I'm not going to get into a take- That's where it came from or an attack on police integrity. It's not an attack, it's facts. Well, it sounds like it's an attack- Hey, look, if you're going to ask a question, ask it properly. But I want to hear the response. Have you finished with Rima Nakli? Yeah. Thank you very much, Mr. Chair, and I want to start by acknowledging the hard work of the committee. It has been a- A very active government, of course, in terms of legislation in the justice space, and this committee has carried the burden of that.

So we've had a very busy time of it. I'd just like to note, for example, a recent milestone with the new offense for stalking and harassment, which took effect on the 26th of May.

from the elected parliament of New Zealand and people of New Zealand that we want to have real consequences for crime. Equally, we've been working away in dealing with if you want to reduce the number of victims of crime, which has been our focus, then an obvious place to start is with that very small group of New Zealanders who create multiple victims over and over again.

A very good place to start is with members of gangs. Gangs are a very small portion of the adult population, far less than 1%, but they're associated with 20% to 25% of violent crime. It will require training, it will require us to work very closely with staff and with stakeholders, and in our case, obviously, with the judiciary. Yeah. But in our case, that's absolutely what I'm expecting. But our trajectory over the next two or three years is very clear that we'll be able to reduce our headcount, and I think do it in a very measured and careful way. And we have been talking to staff about this. I think the ministry staff are not under any illusion that their work worlds are going to change. Bearing in mind that there have been failures in the past, and we don't want to repeat those. But you might like to add. Yeah. Initially, we were targeting middle of this year, beginning of our rollout, leading through until the end of the year. Now we're beginning the rollout in November. We've been running, just in the last few weeks, a lot of workshops where the judiciary have actually been coming and then using the technology as part of the testing process, and that has been-- It's actually very reassuring and very positive because I think we're going to have a system here that will work very well, and it will be within the overarching budget parameters that we've got set for it.

Okay, I've got a couple more steps on this topic. Another one from Camilla Bird. Right, Todd, you said a- Yes. My third area of focus has been to speed up the processes of the courts, because people are waiting years to get a resolution to criminal cases.

And so it's been a real focus of this government to try and turn that around. The good news is we're making progress. And I would make the point, I don't think people who are tuning into this, the global audience and no doubt that we'll have, would be amazed to learn just how rudimentary the current system is.

And so because we've got these great physical folders, and I've been to the back rooms of these courts and it literally is people with these great folders. Now, of course- I've pushed the trolleys. Now, yes. the huge potential for improvement that we've got with Te Aureka. Now, of course, yes, the question that you ask around how can you be assured about it. And so we have a regular process where I'm updated on how it's going and you've got the simple situation in place doing that. And that gives me a reasonable level of confidence. There is always, of course, risk in any large project, and so that has to be acknowledged. This would be one of the most complex IT underpinned change processes in the country.

Yeah. Thank you. I think it's just important because we want this project to succeed. And it sounds to me like, as a justice, as an agency has done this all in the right way. Right. Now we're going to move to a different topic from Timothy Paul. Thanks. Morning. So I'm really interested in some of the real costs in the budget and over the next year as a consequence of political decision making. And I'm going to start with the Treaty Clauses Review, but I am also interested in the costs behind the move on orders.

But we'll start with the Treaty Clause Review. So I guess basic question, do you hope to pass the legislation in full in this term? And if so, what money is set aside in the budget for that bill or that law when it comes into power? Well, in terms of the first question, no, I don't expect we'll pass it before the election.

I expect we'll have the legislation introduced, and we'll have our first reading, and it'll be sent off to a select committee to consider that. In terms of the second, in terms of money set aside, I don't think it necessarily has budget implications. What we're trying to achieve with the Treaty References Review is that over the last 30 years, successive governments have put forth a potpourri of references to the Treaty, and its principles sometimes give effect to, sometimes take account of, sometimes honor, sometimes have regard to,

and they've used different names in terms of the Treaty, Te Tiriti, and a whole bunch of things. And so, the primary exercise is to get some consistency around that. And then ask the question as part of the coalition agreement with New Zealand First, looking at 19 or so of those acts, does it make sense having a Treaty reference at all? And if it does, how can we be clear about what we mean?

And so we've gone through that, set a few aside, because the RMA and the Conservation Act and a number of others have got their own processes, and they've been carried out separately. And we've come to some draft conclusions. We've done some consultation with some iwi groups, and particularly the Iwi Leaders Forum.

What have those iwi leaders said to you about it? Well, been a variety of feedback. I think probably I could generalize and say that the feedback from that quarter has been, "Don't do it, and leave things as they are." But the feedback that we've had from the broader public is that we want to have some clarity about that, and this is a coalition commitment that we're determined to

carry out. If you go through a jury or through a judge-alone trial in terms of legal aid costs, a massive difference.

And so that's part of the equation. So this budget, what we've done is we've put another $30 million into the legal aid budget to keep things going at a reasonable level over the next 12 months. But we've got a furious effort underway across the sector, involving all the key players, to think more radically about how we can get the whole system more efficient and take some pressure off that legal aid budget so that the amount that we put into it achieves more outcomes. I don't know if there's anything you wanted to add

on that, Dave. No, I think that covers it. The legal aid spend, as the minister says, has gone up in the civil jurisdiction. It's gone up by 42% over the last five, six years, by 34% in family, and in criminal, between 23% and 76%. And that's driven by a lot of things.

So the revenue that a legal aid lawyer gets comes both from the fixed rates that there are, and then it comes from the additional costs that they might bring and seek for additional reimbursement from. Yes. The variable REM rates were adjusted in 2022. I think one of the issues that we clearly have to look at as we go into this process is the overall package. So, that will inevitably have to look at how much lawyers get paid and how that payment system is structured.

So how much of it goes through the fixed fee and how much of it is available for amendments to grant, and what REM rate assumptions are made in that space. So, I don't think we can not look at those things as we look through the system because actually, alongside the cost increase end, there are the, what we heard in the triennial review is that this legal workforce was struggling for retention in places.

So we have a situation here that is struggling both in the cost end and its kind of almost its internal capability and sustainability. And we'll have to look at the whole picture in that. But clearly it has to be done within a funding envelope. That will mean we have to be looking at not just at how legal aid is structured at the broader system, so therefore the issues that the minister identified around jury election rates- Yeah... and how some of those are tracking.

We'll have to actually be looking at all of that. But of course, within that, we'll have to be thinking about how much lawyers get paid for all the things that they do. Okay. Very last question on this issue. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you. Going to the sort of systemic issue around legal aid rates, propagation, access to justice, are you working with the police to improve their disclosure system in conjunction with your digitalization work?

The reason I say this, having worked for a decade and course across the entire country, people have to enter a plea if they have initial disclosure under Section 12 of the Criminal Disclosure Act. Initial disclosure is a summary of facts, a conviction history, and a charging document. There's no evidence is required. The election of judge-alone trial or jury trial has to be made at the time of entry of a plea. So the default position is to preserve your position, elect trial by jury, and figure out what the evidence is.

Well, we've had very strong feedback on that topic and the member rightly identifies that you have to deal with this as a system. And my observation coming into it was that you've got police, you've got corrections, you've got the justice department, you've got the judiciary, and you've got the lawyers, and everybody blames everybody else for the problem. And so the best way to deal with that, of course, is to get everybody in the same room, on a regular basis, which is what we do, and say, "Okay, well, how are we going to fix this?" The police say,

well, the defendants say the police aren't ready and the police say, well, the defendants don't turn up, and square them off. So, that's very much the approach we're taking, and so there's been a big effort on the police side through the Police Prosecution Uplift Program- Budget ‘25... which we've invested in Budget ‘25 to improve their levels of being ready. The question around the timing of the election of jury trials and how best to manage that is something that we're considering as part of this broader review, because I

think that that is an issue that's raised. And, so all of those things are very much on the table and part of the discussions. Okay. I'm excited to announce that we're moving to a new topic. Jamie Arbuckle Minister, the biggest event of 2026, and I'm not going to be talking about football or rugby, but it's the election 2026 happening in November.

Yes. $164 million allocated. My questions are around, it's not simply just normal spending because the Auditor-General reviewed in 2023, we did have issues around accounting and processes. So how much of that money's been allocated to remedy some of those issues going into this election?

Now, I'll get the exact figure. I have it in mind, it's about 28 million, but I- Yes... something in that area, but we'll get that confirmed. I wouldn't be able to confirm that right now, the precise breakdown of how much of that money is going to remedial actions and how much of it's going to the general search to get an election year.

But we could come back to the committee with that. We can, but the basic point that you're making is the correct one, that every three years, obviously we have an election. It's a big and expensive business, and there was a number of areas of dissatisfaction with the last election.

Some mistakes were made, and there were debates about a number of things. And so, we had a detailed review of that, and a number of recommendations were made. And the Electoral Commission is rectifying those issues and very focused on it. Another thing that's changed is, of course, that I've appointed a new chair of the Electoral Commission, Simon Moore, who I have a high degree of confidence in.

And we've also extended the board because it was a very unusual structure, where you had the chief electoral officer, the chair, and one other person, three of them, who were effectively the board. And at the last election, through a number of illness, essentially, one of those three was not available.

And so it was quite a strange oversight. We now extended that board to have a wider range of skills available, and I want to thank all the members across the House for supporting that. And I have a high level of confidence that that overall oversight will ensure that we get a very good outcome. Of course, the most important-- Well, there's a huge number of things that the Electoral Commission's focused on, and they're rightly independent, and we have a very good tradition of that in this country, and we hope to determine that.

In terms of the outcome of the election, I can confidently predict that we will win. Supplementary, if I may, Mr. Chair. Yeah. Your public confidence has declined. Yes. And I think the measure they use was something like 76% down to 66. So how important is it for you as minister to see that rise?

Well, very important, and so again, for the benefit of those tuning in and listening in to this, there are various levers that a minister can have when it comes to the Electoral Commission. Bearing in mind, the Electoral Commission, for obvious reasons in a democracy, has a significant degree of independence from government of the day, because obviously we need to have an even playing field when it comes to the running of elections.

But the three levers are the appointment of the chair and the board. Then secondly, I write a letter of expectations to them, focusing on particular things. And one of the things that I focused on in my letter of expectation is that I expect you to have a plan and to be able to demonstrate a plan to increase the levels of trust that New Zealanders have in the Electoral Commission.

And so, that they come back to me with all the things that they're going to do and what they're doing. Of course, the most obvious way to improve that is to have no mistakes, and timely results and all those sorts of things. And then finally, another thing that obviously, put in my letter of expectations was a focus on getting as large a number of New Zealanders enrolled by rip date, so as to reduce the number of special votes. And then thirdly, of course, we have the option of legislative change. And every three years, Parliament makes a few

changes to the rules in order to make the whole system more efficient. And do you think those changes that have been legislated for make more timely decisions at elections? Well, yes. I think it will, and there's been much discussion around that. But the primary reason given for-- Remember that we used to be able to get election outcomes in two weeks, and then it suddenly grew to three.

And all the advice I had was that if we did nothing, it'll be going heading towards four. And people might say, "Well, why does that matter?" Well, it does matter because the history of New Zealand in recent elections is most of them negotiations haven't been able to start on forming a government until the final results, because they shift around quite a lot between election day and the final results.

And that leads to an extended period of uncertainty in our country. And we haven't, to date, had a real crisis during that period, but we never know. We might. And so I think there is a real interest in getting a timely outcome. And so, the main driver of that was the steady and very significant growth of special votes, which on average take about 10 times more time and effort to deal with. And they were just growing and growing and growing.

And thanks to the previous administration, we were sending New Zealanders a very mixed message, which was to say, "Do your legislative duty, which is to be enrolled. Please get enrolled." But by the way, at the same time, we were telling them, "But don't bother because you can do it on election day when you turn up to vote." And most people were hearing the second message and not the first message.

And so fewer and fewer people were enrolled in time. More and more people were enrolling at the box, and that was taking more and more time to get voter, and that's why we're changing it. Right. We'll go to supplementary on the same issue. Yes. Just on that last bit, Minister, can I just check- Yeah... has the minister or ministry investigated automatic registration on a person's 18th, which is both cheaper, as we've seen in other countries, but also a more effective way of getting people to register to vote?

Yes, we have looked at that, and as- The Electoral Commission is looking at a process of how they modernize the election in a range of ways. So they've got more of the electronic enrollment. They'll be doing-- looking at more of that now, and then there's a program of work that will lead in towards the '29 election as well. I'm specifically referring to automatic registration.

So when a person turns 18, they are automatically registered. I think that's something- Bringing in the heavy hitters. There we go. We have looked at it. Yeah. There's one unique feature of the New Zealand electoral system that adds some complexity, which is that we have two rolls. And so one of the things would be how you would make a call as to which roll you automatically register someone on. So that's one of the tricky parts.

But we have looked at it. And that's the one complication. If you automatically enroll people, which roll do you automatically put them on? And a simple answer would be, say, put them all on the- Last question... on the general roll, and then give people the opportunity to shift. But then some people might say, "Well, that's not right." And so that is a technical quirk we've got.

I understand. Thank you for that. But can I just check that other than that quirk, is that something that you're actively- Yes... the ministry's actively pursuing? Yes. All right, cool. Cool. Now, we're coming back to Camilla Belich with a new topic. Thank you. I want to talk about women's imprisonment. This has huge budgetary implications for New Zealand, not only in this budget, but in the future. The women's prison population has almost doubled under this government and is projected to rise by 63% over the next decade.

And I want to ask the minister, is that acceptable to you? And what are you going to be putting in place to ensure that the effect of this is mitigated, especially in relation to not only the fiscal costs, which of course the government will have to meet, but the huge social costs that will arise from having such a large number of women within our criminal justice system. I would remind the minister that the vast majority of women who are imprisoned and who are charged are actually not charged with violent offenses.

Yes. Well, my answers reflect the answers that I gave earlier around general prison population growth. We don't celebrate in more people being in prison. We don't want to have a much larger prison population in our country. We want to see fewer people in prison.

But our focus as a government, of course, is primarily reducing the number of victims of crime. And our goal to reduce the prison population is to reduce crime first, and that will flow through to a lower prison population. I want to make the point, for people tuning in, that it's not easy to get sent to prison in this country.

You do have to make a real effort, and that is either the vast majority of people generally in prison are there because of violent and sexual offending. The member is right to point to a difference in terms of the gender. But to get into prison, one needs to be convicted of a serious offense or a number of repeated offenses that collectively lead to an imprisonment.

So, I'm not happy with it. I would like to have fewer people in prison. But we do want to see a reduction in offending. So supplementary to that, the Crimes Amendment Act that the minister has put forward is one of the pieces of legislation which has been pointed to directly for the number of women in prison likely to increase over the next decade.

So I just wanted to ask his response on that. But also, there is a program in the budget on reducing disproportionality of Maori, and we know that Maori women are a disproportionately high level of the female prison population. So isn't there an innate contradiction in the programs that the minister, and the decisions that the minister is making?

On one hand, he's investing in a program to look at why more Maori are disproportionately imprisoned, and on the other hand, he's passing legislation which is likely to increase the number of those same groups of people who are incarcerated. Well, no, because we're very consistent and clear in our views that there do need to be consequences for crime.

And, in the context of retail crime, there need to be consequences for it. And what we saw as a country, and I think people need to just cast their minds back to the chaos that we were seeing in the retail sector two or three years ago, and where people were encountering people coming out of supermarkets with trolleys full of food and not paying for it, and we needed to do something in that space.

And there are a number of things, but there need to be some consequences for that. And so one of the things in the Crimes Act actually is something that will help on the point that the member is making. It introduces an infringement regime, which is a fine, rather than going through the court system and potentially, if one is convicted over and over again and not getting the message, ending up potentially in prison.

The imposition of a fine at the start hopefully will actually send a clearer message that it won't be tolerated and there are consequences, and deal with the situation. But at the same time, of course, we're very mindful of the disparities in the prison population, and the over-representation of Maori.

And that's why, as a government, and I acknowledge the work of the previous government in this space, and the government before that, and the government before that, it's been a long series of efforts made to reduce that disproportionality. And we've got a lot of programs underway across the justice sector. Most important one being the Te Ao Marama court processes. We've got the alternative pathways for Wahine Maori, such as Ara Poutama, Wahine a Rere ana ki a Pae Hau strategic plan, the Apanui Justice Program, the Te Pae Oranga, a whole series of

programs that are dedicated to providing different approaches. And I think the only conclusion I'd make is that it's hard work, but we're throwing everything we can at it. Right. Dr. Sun, just to finish this topic. Yep. Then we're going to come back to a new one.

Just want to note that the minister would mention at the end, Te Ao Marama has been forced while it's being reviewed. But I want to check in terms of the correlation between the budget and also measurable outcomes. Can the minister give us an international comparable where incarceration has led to a reduction in crime?

Well, I don't have an international matter, but somebody as part of the team might. In terms of measurable progress, I can only point to the most basic and fundamental measurable progress, that we have 46,000 fewer victims of violent and sexual offending in this country than we did two and a half years ago. And that is 46,000 fewer families that have gone through the trauma of being victims of crime. And so that's pretty powerful, I would have thought.

What was the first point that you raised, which I just wondered- Te Ao Marama has been- Te Ao Marama. Yes. So Te Ao Marama. Yes. I suppose it is a contrast between this government and the previous one. So there's been a very significant expansion of the Te Ao Marama courts, which are, just for people tuning in and who might not know what the heck is that, it's a court that very much incorporates Maori approaches to crime and justice and involving the broader community and the whanau, and a more restorative approach to justice.

And that was a court-led system that started off and has been sort of expanded into several different places. Now, there was a projection to keep on expanding it quite vigorously that we inherited. And I made the obvious point, well, hang on a moment.

What evidence do we have of the difference that this is making before we expand? And so what we've decided to do is, and I think starting next year, a thorough assessment. We'll have enough time under our belt to be able to get some measurable progress. And once we've done that, we'll be in a better position to either stay where we are or progress further.

Okay. And we've started the evaluation, but it will be concluded next year because of that period of time. And some of the Te Ao Marama practices are being applied throughout all courts. It's just those where there are particular funding streams attached with community providers that is limited to the eight courts where it's had a more comprehensive rollout.

election? Well, in terms of the precise figures of- Is this your jurisdiction or is it- Yes. It's definitely my-- Yes, and the Ministry of Justice officials- Okay... were involved in the formation of the cabinet paper and the legislation being developed. I don't have an exact figure.

of course, we're a small country where a lot of people have access to people. Mark Smith, the litigant, had access to ministers as well. And so, that's part of how we work. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Minister, throughout the justice there is a lot of references to reducing victims of crime. And you- Reducing the number of victims... the number of victims of crime. And you've spoken a number of times about the stalking legislation, which was quite a milestone move.
